subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Elected Legislature vs. Independent Judiciary in Preserving Limited Government
stimulus: There is continued debate over how to best preserve the democratic ideal of limited government. Develop an argument as to whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 78, Article I of the Constitution of the United States.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority, held accountable through elections, and can check executive overreach through appropriations and oversight.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority and can strike down unconstitutional laws regardless of political pressure.

wrong_examples: There are debates about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government. (Describes a debate — does not take a position)
The executive branch best protects limited government. (Responds to the wrong prompt — the executive branch is not one of the two options in the prompt)
common_traps: Must choose between the ELECTED LEGISLATURE and the INDEPENDENT JUDICIARY — a thesis about the executive branch or a third institution does not respond to the prompt
Limited government means constraining governmental power to protect individual rights — the thesis should connect the chosen institution to this specific democratic ideal
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row A (1 point): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning as to whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government. Examples that earn: 'An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority and are held accountable to the will of the people.' 'An elected legislature is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the legislature to limit the other branches of government.' 'An independent judiciary is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the judiciary to limit the other branches of government.' 'The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority.' Examples that do not earn: 'There are debates about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.' (restates prompt). 'The executive branch best protects limited government.' (does not respond to the prompt).

sample_responses.response: An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority and held accountable to the will of the people through regular elections.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (elected legislature) with a defensible line of reasoning (electoral accountability). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority and can strike down unconstitutional laws.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (independent judiciary) with a defensible line of reasoning (independence from majoritarian pressure). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: There are debates about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.
sample_responses.commentary: Restates the prompt as a debate without taking a position or establishing a line of reasoning. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 51, Federalist 78, Article I, judicial review, Marbury v. Madison, checks and balances, the Bill of Rights, separation of powers, or another relevant concept about limited government.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence with no connection to limited government, legislatures, or the judiciary
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A and evidence only needs to be topically relevant
Judicial review (established in Marbury v. Madison) is a key concept for this prompt — it is how the independent judiciary strikes down unconstitutional laws
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 1 point: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed, any other foundational document, or from course concepts. Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic: 'Article III establishes that federal judicial officials serve for a term of good behavior.' 'Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches.' 'Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments.' 'Article I establishes the legislative branch as well as qualifications for holding office, elections to the office, and the powers of the office.'

sample_responses.response: Article III establishes that federal judicial officials serve for a term of good behavior — giving them lifetime tenure as long as they behave properly.
sample_responses.commentary: Relevant evidence from a foundational document connecting to judicial independence and preserving limited government. Earns Tier 1 regardless of thesis.

sample_responses.response: The Constitutional Convention of 1787 was held in Philadelphia.
sample_responses.commentary: Not relevant to the topic of preserving limited government through the legislature or judiciary. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Federalist 78's argument that the judiciary, being the 'least dangerous branch' because it controls neither the sword nor the purse, can protect constitutional limits without threatening liberty — directly supporting the thesis that an independent judiciary preserves limited government.

wrong_examples: Cites Federalist 78 without explaining Hamilton's argument about judicial independence and how it connects to preserving limited government
common_traps: Federalist 78 (Hamilton) argues for judicial independence and judicial review as the means by which the courts protect the Constitution against legislative overreach — it strongly supports the independent judiciary thesis

Federalist 51 (Madison) argues for checks and balances among all branches — it can support the legislature thesis by showing that Congress's power to check the executive preserves limited government; Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 2 points: Provides one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. To earn 2 points the response must have earned Row A.

sample_responses.response: Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch holds lifetime appointments and is not elected — Hamilton called it the 'least dangerous branch' because it cannot act without the other branches enforcing its decisions. This insulation from political pressure directly supports the thesis that an independent judiciary preserves limited government, because judges can strike down unconstitutional laws without fear of losing their seats.
sample_responses.commentary: Specific Federalist 78 reference tied directly to the judiciary thesis via Hamilton's argument about judicial independence. Earns Tier 2.

sample_responses.response: Federalist No. 51 is about checks and balances. The Supreme Court uses judicial review.
sample_responses.commentary: Names two pieces of evidence but does not explain how either specifically supports the thesis about limited government. Caps at Tier 1. 0 pts for Tier 2.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 78's defense of judicial independence and judicial review to show how an unelected court can nullify unconstitutional laws, AND cites Marbury v. Madison as the historical precedent establishing judicial review — both supporting the thesis that an independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I
Uses Federalist 51 to argue the judiciary should have unlimited power (misreads Federalist 51, which argues for BALANCED power among ALL branches, not judicial supremacy)
common_traps: Article I establishes the legislative branch and its enumerated powers — for the legislature thesis, Article I shows the scope of congressional authority to limit executive power and pass constitutional laws

Federalist 51's famous quote 'ambition must be made to counteract ambition' supports checks and balances — it can be used to argue either branch preserves limited government through its ability to check the other
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 3 points: Provides two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt (Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 78, or Article I). The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Requires Row A.

sample_responses.response: Article I establishes the legislative branch and gives Congress specific powers including the power to tax, spend, and declare war. Additionally, Congress can impeach and remove federal officials, including judges and presidents — which shows how the legislature checks the other branches. Together, these two pieces of evidence support the thesis that an elected legislature preserves limited government by directly constraining what the other branches can do.
sample_responses.commentary: Two specific pieces — Article I (listed foundational document) and impeachment power (course concept) — both supporting the legislature thesis. Earns Tier 3.

sample_responses.response: The Supreme Court uses judicial review to strike down laws that violate the Constitution. Marbury v. Madison established this power.
sample_responses.commentary: Two course concepts but neither is from a listed foundational document (Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I). Caps at Tier 2. 0 pts for Tier 3.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Article I establishes the legislative branch and sets qualifications and powers for members of Congress. Article I preserves limited government because it allows voters to respond to decisions made by the legislature through regular elections, creating an ongoing check on legislative overreach.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments. The judicial branch protects limited government because it is insulated from political pressures that might lead elected officials to expand government power to satisfy popular demands — an elected legislature might grow government to win votes, but judges have no such incentive.

wrong_examples: Federalist 78 says the courts protect the Constitution. (Restates the evidence without explaining the causal link to limited government specifically)
The legislature passes laws, which is important. (Does not connect to the limited government thesis)
common_traps: The reasoning must connect to LIMITED GOVERNMENT specifically — explain how the institution's structure or powers prevent governmental overreach
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row C (1 point): Uses reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports an argument relevant to the prompt. Examples of reasoning: 'Article I establishes the legislative branch as well as qualifications for holding office, elections to the office, and the powers of the office. Article I preserves limited government because it allows voters to respond to decisions made by the legislature through elections.' 'Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches. A legislative override of a presidential veto is one way to check the power of the president and preserve limited government.' 'Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches. The Supreme Court preserves limited government through the use of judicial review, which allows them to decide if laws passed by Congress or executive orders issued by the president are constitutional.' 'Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments. The judicial branch protects limited government because it is insulated from political pressures and can protect against the abuse of power by other branches.'

sample_responses.response: Article I establishes the legislative branch along with qualifications for holding office, regular elections to that office, and the powers of the office. This preserves limited government because it allows voters to respond to decisions made by the legislature through elections — if Congress oversteps, voters can remove members at the next election, creating a built-in mechanism against governmental overreach.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses causal reasoning (elections → accountability → prevention of overreach) to tie Article I to limited government. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Federalist No. 78 explains that judges hold lifetime appointments and are not elected. The judicial branch protects limited government because it is insulated from political pressures and can strike down unconstitutional laws from the legislature or executive without fear of losing its seat — this insulation is what makes the judiciary an effective guardian against overreach.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses causal reasoning (insulation → judicial review without political cost → limited government) to tie Federalist 78 to the thesis. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The legislature is very important in American democracy.
sample_responses.commentary: Asserts a general claim without explaining the causal chain from any specific evidence to the limited-government thesis. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some people might say that an independent judiciary would best preserve limited government because they are not influenced by public opinion. However, an elected legislature better protects limited government because elections hold legislators directly accountable to the people, making it harder for government to grow without the public's consent.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: It can be argued that an elected legislature is more likely to preserve limited government because they are elected by their constituents. However, since elected officials seek popular approval, they may expand government programs to win votes — the independent judiciary, precisely because it is not elected, is better positioned to resist such expansions of government power.

wrong_examples: An elected legislature is also a strong way to protect limited government. (Does not describe an alternate perspective or engage with a counterargument)

Some may argue the executive branch is the best protector of limited government, which I would agree. (The executive branch is not one of the two options in the prompt — does not constitute a relevant alternate perspective)
common_traps: The alternate perspective must be the OTHER option in the prompt (if arguing for elected legislature, the alternate is independent judiciary and vice versa)
Requires Row A — without a thesis, Row D cannot be earned
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row D (1 point): Responds to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute that perspective. Requires Row A. Examples that earn: 'Some people might say that an independent judiciary would best preserve limited government because they are not influenced by public opinion. However, an elected legislature better protects limited government because elections make them responsive to the will of the people.' 'It can be argued that an elected legislature is more likely to preserve limited government because they are elected by their constituents. However, since an independent judiciary is not elected, they are not subject to the will of the people and therefore more likely to preserve limited government.' Responses that do NOT earn: restating the opposite of the thesis; describing an alternate perspective without rebutting; refuting a foundational document rather than an alternate perspective.

sample_responses.response: Some people might say that an independent judiciary would best preserve limited government because judges are not influenced by public opinion and can strike down unconstitutional laws. However, this insulation is precisely the problem — unelected judges have no democratic accountability, so when they expand or contract constitutional rights, citizens have no direct way to correct them. An elected legislature better protects limited government because elections make lawmakers responsive to the will of the people, which is exactly what keeps government constrained and legitimate.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-anchored earned sample for an elected-legislature thesis. Describes the specific opposing view (judicial insulation is a feature) and engages it directly by arguing insulation is actually a defect — addresses the critique head-on rather than pivoting.

sample_responses.response: It can be argued that an elected legislature is more likely to preserve limited government because legislators are accountable to their constituents through regular elections. However, since elected officials depend on popular support, they often expand government programs to win votes — the independent judiciary, precisely because it is not subject to the will of the majority, is better positioned to strike down unconstitutional expansions of government power regardless of political pressure.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-anchored earned sample for an independent-judiciary thesis. Concedes the accountability point and engages it directly by showing why electoral accountability itself creates an incentive to grow government — rebuts the specific critique rather than redirecting.

sample_responses.response: Some people might argue that an independent judiciary is better at preserving limited government because judges are not accountable to the majority. Even though this is true, an elected legislature is still important because it passes laws, represents the people, and is the foundation of American democracy.
sample_responses.commentary: CONCEDE-PLUS-GENERIC-REAFFIRM failure pattern. The response identifies the opposing view (judicial independence from majorities) but the rebuttal pivots to generic reasons the legislature matters (passes laws, represents people, foundation of democracy) rather than engaging the SPECIFIC critique about majoritarian overreach. Does not address why electoral accountability actually preserves limited government better than judicial insulation. 0 pts.
